speaker: interviewer
text: Today is April 10, 1996. The survivor is Slava Fintel, and this is tape four. You were telling us about the search for your brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Zalman starts searching, and with our pleasure of surprise, he found my brother [? Hirska ?] and he brought him with him. They came back to our town. It was very tough for me, since I was almost a grown woman, but maybe in size-- inside, I needed so much. I didn't have any education, because when the war started, I'd just finished the first grade, went into second. I didn't know where to begin. I was mourning so much my family that at times, I felt there is really no right for me to be alive. I didn't know where to turn, what to do. My two brothers got married. My sister was not yet married. She was with me, and I clung to her very much. We decided that it wouldn't be good to stay that long under the Russians, that we should start moving away. Because the war was still going on, and we thought that some-- it will not come out something that it will be for us, that we can really proceed and stay in this forsaken place that we lost everything and everybody. My brother was working for the Russians, and this was very easy to do by bribing and doing. If you bribed somebody, you could get documents, and you can do. And we decided, and we left for Lodz, all of us-- my two brothers, my sister, Tzila, and myself. And their two wives-- I mean my older brother and my younger brother. We came to Lodz. And when I came there, there was organization-- they were sent from Israel, the Bricha. And they-- I went in to an organization. It was Gordonia. And this was only for young children. I was in that place, and I was with them. In the meantime, my sister, Tzila, got married, and my brothers decided to move on with the hope that they will end up in Israel. They start moving also by organization from Israel. The Bricha tried to take them, but we parted. Because Gordonia, the whole-- the whole organization, they took us one day, during the night, through Czechoslovakia, through the-- in the night. And we came through-- we came to Vienna. In Vienna, I-- we stayed again with the same organization, and we had quite fun. We were learning to sing. We were dancing. We were studying some-- some little Hebrew, and some classes were organized for people who didn't know how to read or write. And it was quite exciting. And we were at the American side. And at that time the UNRRA, the Joint, helped to dress us-- I mean, to provide clothes, also food. And life was quite OK. Then I found out that my sister also came to Vienna. And at that time, the moment she came, she was married. I decided, I want to be with her. I left Gordonia and I went with her, and they put us-- brought us into a German small town. They housed all the-- the people who came at that time. And people were coming from everywhere. And they housed us in Germans' homes, and the place was Veksheit. At that time, I decided that I must learn something, that you know, I grow, and without any means of-- of education or anything, and I wanted very much to be a nurse. So I was sent through at that time, through Joint and UNRRA-- organized. Also, it was a hospital, and the place was-- it was not Bogenhausen, it was Veksheit. From Veksheit, I went to Ansbach, a German town. And there was organized a big hospital that was taking care of all the refugees, because many refugees were quite sick. There I studied for two years, and I became-- and of course I had to study not only nursing, but I also had to study the language, and to read and to write. And all together, I became a nurse. This was a time when my two brothers left to go to Israel. And also, Tzila, my sister, decided that she will not go to Israel, that she will go to the United States. So-- and I decided, I'm not going any place until I'm going to be finished with my education.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I finished this particular course, I came back to Wasseralfingen, and there one of my cousins lived still. She wasn't emigrating. I stayed with her for several days until I found out a place that I went to Munich, to another hospital to Bogenhausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, ORT, organization of ORT, collected young girls who they sent to study to Switzerland. This was Interlaken, Ringgenberg by Interlaken, and this was a school of midwife. I studied there for one year, which it was really phenomenal. You can't imagine Switzerland for me, how it looked after all the horrors, and to live through. For the first time, I saw regular high-rise and other buildings, and beautiful, clean, neat. After I finished this particular course, I came back to Munich to the Hospital of Bogenhausen. I worked there for almost a year. And I decided that I would like to go-- to emigrate to Israel. When this happened, we had to go through all kinds of physicals. And at that time, I had to go through a physical, and there was a doctor. And I, of course, went through with flying colors, and the departure was to be two weeks later. This was the place, actually, where my husband found out that some-- one of-- this particular girl is going to be traveling on the same echelon going to Israel from this doctor who gave me the physical. This was a man who was a friend of my husband's. And when we came to the train going to [? Genoa, ?] this is where I met my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Israel. We were traveling on a boat. The name was [? [HEBREW]. ?] It was a cattle boat. It was awful.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were so many people, and there were no beds. They were done like-- you had to sleep on top of the other with-- done from wooden planks. And the-- I was very sick on the boat. I thought, my God, I'm going to die before I see Israel and my two brothers who live there. But I survived that, too. We came-- I came to Israel. My brothers were very happy to have me there. And very shortly afterwards, I married my husband, who I was very much in love. We-- I got the job. By the way, housing at that time in Israel was impossible. You had to pay key money. We didn't have any. We didn't have anything. At that time in Israel, it was Tzena. It was-- everything was rationed. There wasn't food. There wasn't anything that-- but we did it. I got a job. I worked as a nurse in Beilinson. It is near Petah Tikva. My husband got a job because he was a student in Munich, and he finished engineering. He was completely-- he was educated. He got a job in an architectural firm with Karmi. Later on, he became a partner of that firm. We didn't have where to live. I myself went out to friends, to think maybe I can somehow get together some kind of money-- [SIGHS]-- to have enough to pay to find a room or a place where to put our heads after we got married. And I did. I brought some money, and we had enough money to get a-- a beautiful home, a two apart-- a two-room apartment with a kitchen in Petah Tikva by a very prominent family, Gad Machnes. And we gave them the money for the key money, and we moved in to this place. And our son, Dan, was born in Israel. After that, we were very, very happy. We-- our life was really full of happiness, because Israel was blooming and exciting, and life was really beautiful. Many, many friends of my husband's, who all finished their education, they came and they established themselves. And we had a very social and otherwise a very beautiful life.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out-- this was the Sinai War. And when the Russians said that they are going to send their fleet of-- of-- how they say in English, some words? It's--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Volunteers. ?] The volunteers, that the volunteers will be sent, my husband was-- he got so scared that it will be the second annihilation of all of us, that we will all end up like in Europe. He got so scared. He was in the mil-- he was drafted. He was in the military. He came one night. He put me on a plane with my child and sent us off to Brazil. I had a cousin there. And I don't know how I found myself. He put me on the plane with my child with $10 in my pocket. That's all. And we flew to Brazil.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I came, when I was on the plane, I thought to myself, why did I do it? I mean, I thought I will never see my husband again. Never. But God willing, I came to Brazil. My cousin picked me up. I stayed there with our son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two weeks-- no. Three weeks later, the war was finished. My husband got out of the military. He sold whatever we possessed there, and he came and joined me in Brazil. This was another miracle. Because when I saw him at-- I thought that another miracle happened that I was reunited with him. We stayed in Brazil for almost a year. My husband got immediately a job, and he was so good at it that he was asked to become a partner. But at that time, I wanted so badly to go to United States, because my sister Tzila lived here. I wanted very badly to be reunited with her. And we applied, and we had to wait. And we got the papers, and we came to the United States. We came to New York, and immediately my husband got a job. I was, at that time, pregnant with our other son. And we lived in New York. And life wasn't that easy, but it was still beautiful. We were free, happy. He earned a living. After that, he wanted to better himself, and he read an invitation that they are looking for a specialist in Chicago, in Portland Cement Association. We decided that this would be a good thing to look into. My husband accepted the job. We came to Chicago and we lived there the bulk of our life. In the meantime, Steve was born, our daughter Dina was born, and we lived there for almost 16-- 16 years, in Chicago. And this was really the longest time I ever lived in one place, and it really became our home. Our kids succeeded. They all went to school. And I am actually very proud of this achievement, because they are all very well-adjusted, achieved their potential. Right now they are married. They have their own professions, and they have their own children, of whom I am extremely proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you look back upon your experiences, why do you think that you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I recall, I wanted so badly to live. My will to live was constantly so keen. I wanted so badly that I was always the first. I was the first not to sleep and to watch out. I was the first to run. I ran always as fast as I could. When my sister Tzila wanted to lay in that-- in that dump and never to come out, I was the one who dragged her. I was the one to-- to make it sure that she will have that will and go on. I wanted to live. I thought that life was worthwhile living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you often think about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very. I didn't say that before, but the early years, I was plagued by such dreams, by such vivid dreams, that I was constantly running, that I was standing over my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I heard her voice. But I don't know why I never saw her. Until today, I always feel that she is there, but I never saw her voice-- her face. I heard her voice. Never her face. And the dreams were so, so bad. It was so bad that I would wake up-- screams. I felt I was choking. I was constantly choking. And this was for very, very long. But not anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to make this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided because I feel that it's absolutely a responsibility and a must for us survivors to tell, and retell, and retell. Because I would like my children, my grandchildren, and the world never to forget what had happened to us. Because it becomes-- with each year passing, it becomes all watered down and down. We will not live much longer. Lots of our survivors already died, and many will die very soon. And we must. It is an obligation for all of us to do what we can to have it told, our story.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your most painful memory of the Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: My most painful memory-- my mother. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved her so. Do you have a message that you would like to leave future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to leave a message that our children and grandchildren, and their children, shouldn't forget and should try to stay in their religion. Because we shed so much blood for being Jews. And I would like so much for them to continue and not abandon. Because if they abandon, our suffering will go with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Mrs. Fintel, would you be good enough to introduce this very good-looking gentleman that's sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Mark Fintel, whom I am very-- of whom I'm very proud, and very, very much, I would say, still in love.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Fintel, would you like to say a few words about your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I met beautiful Slava in 1950, in June, after I graduated from the Munich University on the way to Israel. We met there, and somehow fell in love immediately. And for days and nights on this cattle boat, I listened to her stories. We married then later in Israel, a year later. Over the 45 years of our marriage, I knew every bit of Slava's stories. But only recently, last May, when we took a trip to Belarus to visit with Piotr, who is still alive, and his family-- and also to dedicate the mass graves in Szarkowszczyna-- I only then could really put together all the pieces, all the little stories, into one-- one complete, all-encompassing story. When we left the grave, the mass grave in Postawy, from where she escaped, it took us three hours on the bad roads of Belarus by car to come to Piotr, which lives near [BELARUSIAN]. I only then realized how a nine-, ten-year-old girl could find her way through those wild places to [BELARUSIAN]. Which is not even a village. It's only a big farm. And seemingly it is only either by the guidance of God or by an extraordinary capability that she could find that place and save herself. Now thinking about it, it is really a wonder how with this past, she could create a warm home, raise three wonderful children, and be a lovable wife for 45 years. And I think this kind of underlines that it was a victory over the evil that created this wonderful, beautiful life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Fintel, before we end, is there anything that you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to add that I have a lot to thank to my husband, because actually, I grew up being the wife of his, of him, and having our beautiful children and grandchildren, whom I absolutely adore. And I hope that our children should have the relationship and the understanding what my husband gave me and what I, in return, gave him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
